questions.question: Which of the following programming languages does Temporal offer an official SDK for?
questions.explanation: Temporal provides official SDKs for several popular languages including Python, along with Go, Java, PHP, .NET, Ruby, and TypeScript. Swift, Rust, and Kotlin do not have official Temporal SDKs listed in the documentation.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of Temporal SDKs?

choices.text: To host and manage cloud infrastructure for your applications
To provide a database for storing application data
To help you build Temporal applications in your preferred programming language
To monitor and log errors in production systems

questions.explanation: Temporal SDKs are libraries that give developers the tools they need to build Temporal applications. They are available in multiple languages so you can work in the language you already know, rather than being forced to learn a new one just to use Temporal.

questions.question: A team is building a Temporal application and needs to choose an SDK. Which of the following languages does Temporal NOT officially support with an SDK?
questions.explanation: Temporal officially supports SDKs for .NET, Go, Java, PHP, Python, Ruby, and TypeScript. Rust does not have an official Temporal SDK. When starting a new Temporal project, choosing a supported SDK ensures access to official documentation, active maintenance, and community support.

questions.question: A polyglot engineering organization wants to write Temporal workers in multiple languages so each team can use their preferred stack. Based on Temporal's official SDK support, which combination of languages would allow all three teams to use official SDKs?
questions.explanation: Java, Ruby, and .NET are all officially supported by Temporal SDKs. Rust, Kotlin, Swift, and C++ do not have official Temporal SDKs. When planning a multi-language Temporal deployment, verifying that each language has an official SDK ensures consistent support, versioning, and access to the full Temporal feature set across all workers.
